1:
"What shall we say then that Abraham our father, as pertaining to the flesh, hath found?" The witnesses note Paul's turn to Abraham as the great test case. On the question, the grammatical voices note the method. Robertson and Barnes note Paul appeals to Abraham — the father of the Jewish nation, the supreme example of piety — to prove that justification is by faith, not works; settling the matter from the case the Jews most revered.

On "as pertaining to the flesh," the witnesses note the reference. Most take it of Abraham's own efforts or natural descent — what did Abraham find or attain by his own works of the flesh? The answer (v.2) is, nothing to glory in before God.

The eastern Chrysostom and Theodoret note Paul, having proved justification by faith, confirms it from the patriarch, that the Jew cannot object. The verse displays the turn to Abraham as the decisive test of justification.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul turns to Abraham — the father of the nation, the supreme example of piety — to prove justification is by faith, not works (Robertson, Barnes), asking what Abraham found "as pertaining to the flesh" (by his own works or natural efforts), the answer being nothing to glory in before God (v.2). Chrysostom and Theodoret note Paul confirms his doctrine from the patriarch the Jews revered. The verse displays the turn to Abraham. The verse marks the method: the decisive test case. The verse displays the reverence: Abraham the father. The verse displays the question: what did he find by the flesh? The turn to Abraham displays Paul's appeal to the case the Jews most honored. The verse displays the strategy: proving from Abraham. The verse displays the foreshadowed answer: nothing by works. The verse displays the authority: the patriarch's example. The turn to Abraham as the decisive test of justification displays Paul's masterful strategy: proving justification by faith from the very father of the Jewish nation, the supreme example of piety, whom no Jew could gainsay. The verse displays the apostle settling the great question from the case his readers most revered, that the doctrine of justification by faith might be established beyond dispute. The verse sets up the proof: Abraham was not justified by works (v.2).
2:
"For if Abraham were justified by works, he hath whereof to glory; but not before God." The witnesses note the argument from boasting. On "if justified by works, he hath whereof to glory," the grammatical voices note the hypothesis. Barnes and Robertson note that if Abraham were justified by works, he would have ground for boasting — a claim to merit.

On "but not before God," the witnesses note the decisive limit. Whatever Abraham might boast before men, he had nothing to glory in before God — for justification by works is excluded (3:27). Before God, all boasting is silenced.

The Reformed Calvin notes the proof: if Abraham was justified by embracing God's mercy by faith, he had nothing to glory in, bringing only a confession of his misery. The verse displays the argument: no boasting before God.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: if Abraham were justified by works, he would have ground for boasting (a claim to merit, Barnes, Robertson) — but not before God, where all boasting is silenced, for justification by works is excluded (3:27). Calvin notes that if Abraham was justified by embracing God's mercy by faith, he had nothing to glory in, bringing only a confession of his misery. The verse displays the argument: no boasting before God. The verse marks the hypothesis: if by works, then boasting. The verse displays the limit: but not before God. The verse displays the exclusion: justification by works ruled out. The argument from boasting displays the impossibility of glorying before God. The verse displays the silencing: no merit before God. The verse displays the confession: only misery to bring. The verse displays the principle: works exclude grace. The argument from boasting — that if Abraham were justified by works he would have whereof to glory, but not before God — displays the impossibility of self-glory before God: even the great patriarch, before the divine tribunal, had nothing to boast of, but only a confession of his need of mercy. The verse displays the great principle that justification by works would leave room for boasting, which the gospel forever excludes, and sets up the scriptural proof that Abraham was justified by faith (v.3).
3:
"For what saith the scripture? Abraham believed God, and it was counted unto him for righteousness." The witnesses note the key text on imputation (Genesis 15:6). On "Abraham believed God," the grammatical voices note the faith. Barnes and Calvin note Abraham's faith was his strong, unwavering confidence in God's promise (of a posterity, and, included in it, the covenant of grace and Christ).

On "counted for righteousness" (elogisthe), the witnesses note imputation. Robertson notes the verb means to set down on the credit side of the ledger — Abraham's believing was reckoned, set down, for righteousness. The same word is rendered "counted," "reckoned," "imputed."

On what was counted, the witnesses divide. Barnes argues the act of believing was counted for righteousness (faith the instrument, not a transfer); the Reformed Calvin and Ellicott note the imputation is forensic — faith treated as equivalent to righteousness because it lays hold on Christ, whose righteousness is the ground; not faith as merit but as the means of receiving Christ's righteousness. The verse displays the key text: Abraham's faith counted for righteousness.

The witnesses agree the key text (Genesis 15:6) is decisive: Abraham believed God (his strong confidence in the promise, including the covenant of grace and Christ, Barnes, Calvin), and it was counted (elogisthe, reckoned, set on the credit side, Robertson) for righteousness. They divide on what was counted: Barnes holds the act of believing was reckoned for righteousness (faith the instrument, not a transfer); Calvin and Ellicott hold a forensic imputation — faith counted as righteousness because it lays hold on Christ, whose righteousness is the ground, faith not a merit but the means of receiving it. The verse displays the key text. The verse marks the faith: Abraham believed God. The verse displays the imputation: counted for righteousness. The verse displays the debate: the act of faith, or faith laying hold on Christ. The key text displays the foundation of justification by faith. The verse displays the reckoning: faith credited as righteousness. The verse displays the instrument: faith receiving. The verse displays the ground: God's mercy in Christ. The key text — Abraham believed God, and it was counted to him for righteousness — displays the very foundation of justification by faith, the scriptural proof to which Paul returns again and again. The verse displays the great doctrine of imputation: that faith, laying hold on God's mercy in Christ, is reckoned for righteousness, not as a meritorious work but as the appointed means of receiving the righteousness of God. The verse sets up the contrast of grace and debt (vv.4-5).
4:
"Now to him that worketh is the reward not reckoned of grace, but of debt." The witnesses note the principle of works: reward as debt. On "to him that worketh," the grammatical voices note the worker. Robertson and Calvin note the "worker" is one who seeks to merit by his works — not the believer who does good, but one who claims wages as his due.

On "reward... of debt," the witnesses note the principle. Barnes and Theodoret note that to one who works, the reward (wages, misthos) is reckoned not as a favour but as a debt — something earned and owed, claimable by right.

The Reformed Calvin notes the contrast: if one merits by works, the reward is his due, not grace; but faith receives righteousness as a free gift, not as wages. The verse displays the principle of works: reward reckoned as debt, not grace.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: to the worker (one who seeks to merit by works, claiming wages as his due, Robertson, Calvin) the reward (misthos, wages) is reckoned not of grace but of debt — earned and owed, claimable by right (Barnes, Theodoret). Calvin notes the contrast: if one merits by works, the reward is his due, not grace; but faith receives righteousness as a free gift. The verse displays the principle of works. The verse marks the worker: claiming merit. The verse displays the reward: reckoned as debt. The verse displays the principle: wages owed, not grace. The verse displays the contrast: debt vs grace. The principle of works displays why justification cannot be by works. The verse displays the debt: the worker's claim. The verse displays the exclusion: no grace where merit. The verse displays the foundation: grace and debt opposed. The principle of works — that to him who works the reward is reckoned as debt, not grace — displays why justification cannot be by works: for if it were earned, it would be a debt owed, leaving no room for grace. The verse displays the great antithesis between grace and merit, debt and gift, that runs through Paul's gospel, and sets up the opposite principle: justification of the one who works not but believes (v.5).
5:
"But to him that worketh not, but believeth on him that justifieth the ungodly, his faith is counted for righteousness." The witnesses note the great statement: God justifies the ungodly. On "to him that worketh not, but believeth," the grammatical voices note the contrast. Barnes notes this is one who does not rely on works but believes — receiving righteousness as a gift, not wages.

On "him that justifieth the ungodly," the witnesses note the heart of the gospel. Robertson cites Denney: "The whole Pauline gospel could be summed up in this one word — God who justifies the ungodly." God justifies not the righteous but the impious — pardoning and accepting the sinner who believes.

The Reformed Calvin and wesleyan-arminian Wesley note the profound truth: God justifies the ungodly, so no one seeks the righteousness of faith but he who feels himself ungodly; holiness does not precede justification, for if a man could be made holy first, it would set his justification aside. The verse displays the great statement: faith counted for righteousness to him who believes on the God who justifies the ungodly.

The witnesses agree this is the heart of the gospel: to him who works not but believes on him who justifies the ungodly, his faith is counted for righteousness — God justifying not the righteous but the impious (Robertson, Denney: "the whole Pauline gospel in one word"), pardoning the sinner who believes (Barnes). Calvin and Wesley note the profound truth: none seeks the righteousness of faith but he who feels himself ungodly; holiness does not precede justification, for to be made holy first would set justification aside. The verse displays the great statement. The verse marks the contrast: works not, but believes. The verse displays the gospel: God justifies the ungodly. The verse displays the order: faith, then holiness. The verse displays the wonder: the impious justified. The great statement displays the astonishing grace of the gospel. The verse displays the object: God who justifies the ungodly. The verse displays the gift: faith counted for righteousness. The verse displays the truth: justification of sinners. That God justifies the ungodly, counting the believer's faith for righteousness, displays the most astonishing and central truth of the gospel: God pardons and accepts not the righteous but the impious sinner who believes, justifying him freely by grace. The verse displays the whole Pauline gospel in a single phrase, and the profound truth that holiness follows, not precedes, justification — for God justifies the ungodly. The verse sets up David's confirming witness (vv.6-8).
6:
"Even as David also describeth the blessedness of the man, unto whom God imputeth righteousness without works," The witnesses note David's confirming witness. On "even as David," the grammatical voices note the second proof. Barnes and Robertson note Paul, having proved his point from Abraham, adds David — the most eminent king — citing Psalm 32 to show the same doctrine of justification without works.

On "imputeth righteousness without works," the witnesses note the key phrase. The blessedness David describes is of the man to whom God imputes (reckons) righteousness apart from works — treating him as righteous by grace, not by his deeds.

The Reformed Calvin notes the substance: justification with Paul is the remission of sins; David describes blessedness as forgiveness, not works — and the works are not limited to ceremonies but excluded entirely. The verse displays David's witness: righteousness imputed without works.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul adds David (the most eminent king) as a second witness (Barnes, Robertson), citing Psalm 32 to show the blessedness of the man to whom God imputes righteousness without works — treating him as righteous by grace, not deeds. Calvin notes justification is the remission of sins; David describes blessedness as forgiveness, the works excluded entirely (not merely ceremonies). The verse displays David's witness. The verse marks the second proof: David after Abraham. The verse displays the key phrase: righteousness imputed without works. The verse displays the blessedness: the forgiven man. The verse displays the exclusion: without works. David's witness displays the confirmation of justification by grace. The verse displays the imputation: righteousness reckoned. The verse displays the apartness: without works. The verse displays the substance: remission of sins. David's witness — the blessedness of the man to whom God imputes righteousness without works — displays the confirmation of justification by grace from a second great Old Testament figure, the most eminent king. The verse displays the doctrine that justification is the gracious imputation of righteousness apart from works, which David himself, after long service of God, found to be the only ground of blessedness. The verse sets up the citation of Psalm 32 (vv.7-8).
7:
"Saying, Blessed are they whose iniquities are forgiven, and whose sins are covered." The witnesses note the blessedness of forgiveness (Psalm 32:1). On "blessed are they," the grammatical voices note the felicitation. Barnes and Robertson note David pronounces blessed (happy, highly favoured) those whose sins are dealt with by God's mercy.

On "iniquities forgiven... sins covered," the witnesses note the two-fold mercy. Barnes notes "covered" means hidden from God's view, no more remembered — forgiveness, not the atonement here but the act of pardoning. Iniquities forgiven (the debt remitted) and sins covered (concealed, removed from sight).

The witnesses note the connection: this blessedness is the same as imputed righteousness (v.6) — justification is the forgiveness of sins. The verse displays the blessedness of forgiveness.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: David pronounces blessed (happy, highly favoured) those whose iniquities are forgiven (the debt remitted) and whose sins are covered (hidden from God's view, no more remembered, Barnes, Robertson) — the two-fold mercy of pardon. This blessedness is the same as imputed righteousness (v.6): justification is the forgiveness of sins. The verse displays the blessedness of forgiveness. The verse marks the felicitation: blessed are they. The verse displays the mercy: iniquities forgiven. The verse displays the covering: sins concealed. The verse displays the connection: pardon is justification. The blessedness of forgiveness displays the gracious nature of justification. The verse displays the remission: the debt forgiven. The verse displays the concealment: sins covered. The verse displays the happiness: of the pardoned. The blessedness of forgiveness — those whose iniquities are forgiven and whose sins are covered — displays the gracious nature of justification as the remission of sins: God pardoning the debt and hiding the sin from his view, no more to remember it. The verse displays the happiness of the forgiven man, the same blessedness as the imputation of righteousness without works, and continues the citation of Psalm 32 (v.8). The covering of sin under the Old Testament foreshadowed the perfect covering wrought by Christ, in whom the believer is hidden from the condemning eye of the law and beheld only in the imputed righteousness of the Redeemer.
8:
"Blessed is the man to whom the Lord will not impute sin." The brief verse continues the citation of Psalm 32. On "to whom the Lord will not impute sin," the grammatical voices note the non-imputation. Barnes and Robertson note the blessedness of the man whose sin God will not reckon, not charge to his account — the negative side of imputed righteousness.

On the connection with imputation, the witnesses note the parallel. The non-imputation of sin (v.8) corresponds to the imputation of righteousness (v.6) — God not charging sin, and reckoning righteousness; the two sides of justification.

The Reformed Calvin notes David stops short of "in whose spirit is no guile" because his subject is justification, not sanctification — marking the difference; the blessedness is the gratuitous non-imputation of sin. The verse displays the blessedness of the man whose sin is not imputed.

The very brief comment matches the briefest verse. The witnesses agree: blessed is the man to whom the Lord will not impute (reckon, charge) sin — the negative side of imputed righteousness, God not charging sin to his account (Barnes, Robertson), corresponding to the imputation of righteousness (v.6). Calvin notes David's subject is justification, not sanctification (stopping short of "no guile"); the blessedness is the gratuitous non-imputation of sin. The verse displays the blessedness of non-imputation. The verse marks the negative side: sin not reckoned. The verse displays the parallel: non-imputation of sin, imputation of righteousness. The verse displays the gratuity: sin freely not charged. The verse displays the justification: God not reckoning sin. The blessedness of non-imputation displays the gracious foundation of justification. The verse displays the pardon: sin not charged. The verse displays the two sides: sin not reckoned, righteousness reckoned. The verse displays the gratuitous mercy. The blessedness of the man to whom the Lord will not impute sin displays the gracious foundation of justification: God freely declining to charge sin to the believer's account. The verse displays the negative side of the great doctrine of imputation — the non-reckoning of sin answering to the reckoning of righteousness — and the gratuitous mercy by which the sinner is accounted blessed. The verse closes the citation of David and sets up the question of circumcision (v.9).
9:
"Cometh this blessedness then upon the circumcision only, or upon the uncircumcision also? for we say that faith was reckoned to Abraham for righteousness." The witnesses note the question of circumcision. On the question, the grammatical voices note the turn. Barnes and Robertson note Paul asks whether this blessedness (of imputed righteousness) belongs to the circumcised (Jews) only, or also to the uncircumcised (Gentiles) — pressing the universality of justification by faith.

On "faith reckoned to Abraham," the witnesses note the recall. Paul restates the key fact (v.3) to set up the decisive argument: when was Abraham's faith reckoned — before or after circumcision? (v.10).

The puritan Henry notes the point: the blessedness is not tied to circumcision, for Abraham was justified by faith — and the question prepares to show it extends to the uncircumcised too. The verse displays the question: is this blessedness for the circumcised only?

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul asks whether the blessedness of imputed righteousness belongs to the circumcised (Jews) only or also to the uncircumcised (Gentiles), pressing the universality of justification by faith (Barnes, Robertson), and restates the key fact (v.3) — faith reckoned to Abraham — to set up the decisive argument (v.10). Henry notes the blessedness is not tied to circumcision. The verse displays the question of circumcision. The verse marks the turn: circumcised only, or also uncircumcised? The verse displays the recall: faith reckoned to Abraham. The verse displays the press: the universality of faith. The question of circumcision displays the reach of justification beyond the Jews. The verse displays the inquiry: who shares the blessedness? The verse displays the setup: when was faith reckoned? The verse displays the universality: faith for all. The question whether this blessedness comes upon the circumcised only or the uncircumcised also displays Paul's argument for the universality of justification by faith: since Abraham's faith was reckoned for righteousness, the question is whether that blessedness is confined to the circumcised. The verse displays the apostle pressing toward the conclusion that justification by faith is open to Gentile as well as Jew, and sets up the decisive proof from the timing of Abraham's justification (v.10).
10:
"How was it then reckoned? when he was in circumcision, or in uncircumcision? Not in circumcision, but in uncircumcision." The witnesses note the decisive proof from timing. On the question, the grammatical voices note the argument. Barnes and Robertson note Paul asks when Abraham's faith was reckoned for righteousness — and answers: not when he was circumcised, but while still uncircumcised (Genesis 15 preceding Genesis 17 by years).

On the conclusion, the witnesses note the force. Abraham was justified by faith while still a Gentile (uncircumcised) — therefore justification by faith does not depend on circumcision, and is open to the uncircumcised.

The witnesses note the decisive point: the historical sequence proves that righteousness was reckoned to Abraham before circumcision, so faith, not circumcision, is the ground. The verse displays the decisive proof: Abraham justified while uncircumcised.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul asks when Abraham's faith was reckoned — and answers, not when circumcised but while still uncircumcised (Genesis 15 preceding Genesis 17 by years, Barnes, Robertson); Abraham was justified by faith while still a Gentile, so justification does not depend on circumcision and is open to the uncircumcised. The verse displays the decisive proof from timing. The verse marks the question: when was faith reckoned? The verse displays the answer: in uncircumcision. The verse displays the conclusion: justification before circumcision. The verse displays the force: faith, not circumcision, the ground. The decisive proof displays the independence of justification from circumcision. The verse displays the sequence: faith first, circumcision after. The verse displays the openness: to the uncircumcised. The verse displays the ground: faith alone. The decisive proof from timing — Abraham's faith reckoned for righteousness while he was yet uncircumcised — displays the independence of justification from circumcision: since Abraham was justified by faith years before he was circumcised, faith and not circumcision is the ground, and justification is open to Gentile as well as Jew. The verse displays the unanswerable historical argument from the order of Genesis, and sets up the meaning of circumcision as a seal (v.11). The order of the Genesis narrative thus becomes an unanswerable proof, fixing the priority of faith over every external rite and confirming that the door of justification stands open to Jew and Gentile on the one footing of believing.
11:
"And he received the sign of circumcision, a seal of the righteousness of the faith which he had yet being uncircumcised: that he might be the father of all them that believe, though they be not circumcised; that righteousness might be imputed unto them also:" The witnesses note circumcision as the seal of faith's righteousness, and Abraham father of all believers. On "a seal of the righteousness of faith," the grammatical voices note the meaning. Barnes and Robertson note circumcision was not the cause but a sign and seal — confirming the righteousness Abraham already had by faith while uncircumcised; a seal authenticating the prior gift.

On "father of all them that believe, though not circumcised," the witnesses note the universality. Abraham is the father of all believers, circumcised or not — the Gentiles who believe are his true children, that righteousness might be imputed to them also.

The eastern Theodoret and Reformed Calvin note the point: circumcision sealed, did not produce, righteousness; and Abraham's fatherhood extends to all who walk in his faith. The verse displays circumcision the seal, Abraham the father of all believers.

The witnesses agree: circumcision was a sign and seal of the righteousness of faith Abraham already had while uncircumcised (not the cause but a confirming seal authenticating the prior gift, Barnes, Robertson), that he might be the father of all who believe, circumcised or not — the believing Gentiles his true children, that righteousness might be imputed to them also. Theodoret and Calvin note circumcision sealed, did not produce, righteousness; Abraham's fatherhood extends to all who walk in his faith. The verse displays circumcision the seal, Abraham the father of all believers. The verse marks the meaning: a seal of faith's righteousness. The verse displays the order: faith first, circumcision sealing. The verse displays the fatherhood: of all believers. The verse displays the universality: Gentiles his children. Circumcision the seal displays the subordination of the rite to faith. The verse displays the seal: confirming the prior gift. The verse displays the fatherhood: extending to all believers. The verse displays the imputation: to the uncircumcised also. Circumcision as the seal of the righteousness of faith, and Abraham as the father of all believers, displays the true relation of the sign to the substance: circumcision sealed but did not produce the righteousness Abraham had by faith, and his fatherhood extends to all who believe, circumcised or not. The verse displays the great truth that the believing Gentiles are Abraham's true children, sharing the imputed righteousness of faith. The verse continues the theme of Abraham's universal fatherhood (v.12).
12:
"And the father of circumcision to them who are not of the circumcision only, but who also walk in the steps of that faith of our father Abraham, which he had being yet uncircumcised." The witnesses note Abraham father of the circumcised who also believe. On "father of circumcision," the grammatical voices note the qualification. Barnes and Robertson note Abraham is the father of the circumcised — but not merely by physical descent or the rite; only of those who also walk in the steps of his faith.

On "who walk in the steps of that faith," the witnesses note the condition. The true children of Abraham among the Jews are not those merely circumcised, but those who imitate his faith — faith, not the flesh, makes the true child.

The Reformed Calvin and eastern Theodoret note the point: circumcision alone does not make one Abraham's child; only faith does. The verse displays Abraham father of the circumcised who also believe.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Abraham is the father of the circumcised — but not merely by the rite or physical descent; only of those who also walk in the steps of his faith (Barnes, Robertson), faith and not the flesh making the true child. Calvin and Theodoret note circumcision alone does not make one Abraham's child; only faith does. The verse displays Abraham father of the circumcised who also believe. The verse marks the qualification: not the rite alone. The verse displays the condition: walking in his faith. The verse displays the truth: faith makes the true child. The verse displays the limit: not the flesh but faith. Abraham father of the believing circumcised displays the priority of faith over the rite. The verse displays the imitation: walking in his steps. The verse displays the exclusion: not circumcision alone. The verse displays the true children: those of faith. That Abraham is the father of the circumcised only insofar as they also walk in the steps of his faith displays the great truth that faith, not the flesh or the rite, makes the true child of Abraham: even among the Jews, only those who imitate his faith are his true children. The verse displays the consistent principle that justification and sonship are by faith, completing the theme of Abraham's two-fold fatherhood (of believing Gentiles and believing Jews). The verse sets up the promise through faith, not law (v.13).
13:
"For the promise, that he should be the heir of the world, was not to Abraham, or to his seed, through the law, but through the righteousness of faith." The witnesses note the promise through faith, not law. On "the promise... heir of the world," the grammatical voices note the scope. Barnes and Robertson note the promise to Abraham (that he and his seed should inherit the world — fulfilled in the universal blessing through Christ and the worldwide family of faith) was a vast promise.

On "not through the law, but through the righteousness of faith," the witnesses note the ground. The promise came not on the condition of law-keeping (the law not yet given) but through the righteousness of faith — by grace, received by believing.

The puritan Henry and Reformed Calvin note the point: the inheritance rests on the promise and faith, not the law; for the promise preceded the law by centuries. The verse displays the promise through faith, not law.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the promise that Abraham and his seed should be heir of the world (fulfilled in the universal blessing through Christ and the worldwide family of faith, Barnes, Robertson) came not through the law (not yet given) but through the righteousness of faith — by grace, received by believing. Henry and Calvin note the inheritance rests on the promise and faith, not the law, which came centuries later. The verse displays the promise through faith. The verse marks the scope: heir of the world. The verse displays the ground: the righteousness of faith. The verse displays the exclusion: not through the law. The verse displays the timing: promise before law. The promise through faith displays the inheritance grounded in grace, not law. The verse displays the vastness: heir of the world. The verse displays the means: faith, not law. The verse displays the priority: promise before law. The promise that Abraham should be heir of the world, given through the righteousness of faith and not through the law, displays the inheritance grounded in grace rather than law-keeping: the promise preceded the law by centuries and rested on faith. The verse displays the great truth that the blessing and inheritance come by the promise received through faith, fulfilled in the worldwide family of believers in Christ. The verse sets up the argument that law and promise are incompatible as grounds (vv.14-15).
14:
"For if they which are of the law be heirs, faith is made void, and the promise made of none effect:" The witnesses note the incompatibility of law and promise. On "if they of the law be heirs," the grammatical voices note the hypothesis. Barnes and Robertson note that if the inheritance were by the law (law-keeping), then faith would be emptied (made void) and the promise nullified.

On "faith made void... promise of none effect," the witnesses note the consequence. Law and promise are mutually exclusive as grounds: if heirship is by law, faith is useless and the promise (which is free) is destroyed.

The Reformed Calvin notes the principle: the law and faith cannot coexist as grounds of justification; to introduce law is to abolish the promise and faith. The verse displays the incompatibility: law-heirship empties faith and the promise.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: if the inheritance were by the law (law-keeping), faith would be emptied (made void) and the promise nullified (Barnes, Robertson) — law and promise mutually exclusive as grounds: if heirship is by law, faith is useless and the free promise destroyed. Calvin notes the law and faith cannot coexist as grounds of justification; to introduce law is to abolish promise and faith. The verse displays the incompatibility of law and promise. The verse marks the hypothesis: if heirs by law. The verse displays the consequence: faith made void. The verse displays the nullification: promise of none effect. The verse displays the exclusion: law and promise opposed. The incompatibility displays why the inheritance cannot be by law. The verse displays the emptying: faith made void. The verse displays the destruction: the promise nullified. The verse displays the principle: law and grace exclusive. The incompatibility of law and promise — that if heirship were by law, faith would be made void and the promise of none effect — displays why the inheritance cannot rest on law-keeping: law and the free promise are mutually exclusive as grounds, so to make the law the ground is to empty faith and destroy the promise. The verse displays the great antithesis between law and grace, and sets up the reason the law cannot be the ground: it works wrath (v.15).
15:
"Because the law worketh wrath: for where no law is, there is no transgression." The witnesses note why the law cannot be the ground: it works wrath. On "the law worketh wrath," the grammatical voices note the function. Barnes and Robertson note the law, being violated by all, produces wrath (condemnation and punishment) — it cannot give the inheritance but only exposes guilt and brings the curse.

On "where no law is, there is no transgression," the witnesses note the principle. Transgression presupposes law; the law, by defining sin, turns it into transgression — formal violation of a known command. Without law there is sin but not transgression in the formal sense.

The Reformed Calvin and wesleyan-arminian Wesley note the point: the law brings wrath because none keep it; so the promise must rest on grace, not law. The verse displays the law working wrath, not inheritance.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the law, being violated by all, works wrath (condemnation and punishment, Barnes, Robertson) — it cannot give the inheritance but exposes guilt and brings the curse; and where no law is, there is no transgression (transgression presupposing law, the law turning sin into formal violation of a known command). Calvin and Wesley note the law brings wrath because none keep it, so the promise must rest on grace. The verse displays the law working wrath. The verse marks the function: the law works wrath. The verse displays the reason: all violate it. The verse displays the principle: no law, no transgression. The verse displays the conclusion: grace, not law. The law working wrath displays why it cannot be the ground of inheritance. The verse displays the condemnation: wrath produced. The verse displays the definition: law turns sin to transgression. The verse displays the necessity: the promise by grace. The law working wrath, not inheritance — for where no law is there is no transgression — displays why the promise cannot rest on the law: the law, being violated by all, brings only condemnation and the curse, and by defining sin turns it into formal transgression. The verse displays the great truth that the law's office is to expose guilt and work wrath, driving sinners to the grace of the promise. The verse sets up the conclusion: the promise is of faith, by grace (v.16).
16:
"Therefore it is of faith, that it might be by grace; to the end the promise might be sure to all the seed; not to that only which is of the law, but to that also which is of the faith of Abraham; who is the father of us all," The witnesses note the conclusion: of faith, by grace, sure to all the seed. On "of faith, that it might be by grace," the grammatical voices note the connection. Barnes, Robertson, and Calvin note the inheritance is of faith precisely so that it might be by grace — faith and grace go together, as law and debt do; the free promise requires faith as its means.

On "sure to all the seed," the witnesses note the certainty and scope. Because it is by grace through faith, the promise is firm and certain to all the seed — both Jews (of the law) and Gentiles (of Abraham's faith), the whole family of believers.

On "the father of us all," the eastern Theodoret and others note Abraham's universal fatherhood: the father of all believers, Jew and Gentile. The verse displays the conclusion: of faith, by grace, sure to all the seed.

The witnesses agree: the inheritance is of faith that it might be by grace (faith and grace going together, as law and debt do; the free promise requiring faith, Barnes, Robertson, Calvin), to the end the promise might be sure to all the seed — firm and certain to both Jews (of the law) and Gentiles (of Abraham's faith), Abraham the father of us all (Theodoret). The verse displays the conclusion: of faith, by grace, sure to all. The verse marks the connection: faith so that grace. The verse displays the certainty: the promise sure. The verse displays the scope: all the seed. The verse displays the fatherhood: of us all. The conclusion displays the harmony of faith, grace, and the sure promise. The verse displays the pairing: faith and grace. The verse displays the firmness: the promise certain. The verse displays the universality: all believers. The conclusion that the inheritance is of faith that it might be by grace, sure to all the seed, displays the beautiful harmony of faith, grace, and the certainty of the promise: because it rests on grace received by faith, the promise is firm and sure to the whole family of believers, Jew and Gentile, of whom Abraham is the father of us all. The verse displays the security of the gospel inheritance grounded in grace, and sets up Abraham's faith in the God who quickens the dead (v.17).
17:
"(As it is written, I have made thee a father of many nations,) before him whom he believed, even God, who quickeneth the dead, and calleth those things which be not as though they were." The witnesses note Abraham's faith in the life-giving, creating God. On "father of many nations," the grammatical voices note the promise (Genesis 17:5). Abraham was made the father of many nations — fulfilled in the spiritual seed, the worldwide family of faith.

On "God, who quickeneth the dead," the witnesses note the object of faith. Barnes and Robertson note Abraham believed God as the one who gives life to the dead (raising up seed from his own and Sarah's dead bodies) — the God of resurrection.

On "calleth those things which be not as though they were," the witnesses note the creative power. The puritan Henry and Reformed Calvin note God calls into being what does not exist — the Creator's power; Abraham's faith rested on God's omnipotence to fulfill the promise against all natural impossibility. The verse displays Abraham's faith in the God who raises the dead and creates from nothing.

The witnesses agree: Abraham was made the father of many nations (Genesis 17:5, fulfilled in the worldwide family of faith), believing God as the one who quickens the dead (raising up seed from his own and Sarah's dead bodies, the God of resurrection, Barnes, Robertson) and calls things that be not as though they were (the Creator's power, calling into being what does not exist, Henry, Calvin). Abraham's faith rested on God's omnipotence against all natural impossibility. The verse displays Abraham's faith in the life-giving, creating God. The verse marks the promise: father of many nations. The verse displays the object: God who quickens the dead. The verse displays the power: calling things that be not. The verse displays the ground: God's omnipotence. Abraham's faith displays its grand object: the God of resurrection and creation. The verse displays the resurrection power: quickening the dead. The verse displays the creative power: calling from nothing. The verse displays the foundation: God's omnipotence. Abraham's faith in the God who quickens the dead and calls things that be not as though they were displays the grand object of saving faith: the omnipotent God of resurrection and creation, able to fulfill his promise against every natural impossibility. The verse displays the nature of true faith, resting not on visible probability but on the creative, life-giving power of God — the same God who raised Jesus from the dead (v.24). The verse sets up the description of Abraham's strong faith (vv.18-22).
18:
"Who against hope believed in hope, that he might become the father of many nations, according to that which was spoken, So shall thy seed be." The witnesses note Abraham's faith against hope. On "against hope believed in hope," the grammatical voices note the paradox. Barnes and Robertson note Abraham, against all natural hope (his and Sarah's age making offspring impossible), believed in hope (resting on God's promise) — faith triumphing over natural impossibility.

On "that he might become the father of many nations," the witnesses note the object. Abraham believed the promise that he should have a numerous seed (Genesis 15:5, "so shall thy seed be") — trusting God's word against the evidence of nature.

The eastern Theodoret and Reformed Calvin note the strength: Abraham's faith rose above natural reason, resting wholly on the promise. The verse displays Abraham's faith against hope.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Abraham, against all natural hope (his and Sarah's age making offspring impossible), believed in hope (resting on God's promise) — faith triumphing over natural impossibility (Barnes, Robertson) — that he might become the father of many nations, trusting the promise "so shall thy seed be" (Genesis 15:5) against the evidence of nature. Theodoret and Calvin note Abraham's faith rose above natural reason, resting wholly on the promise. The verse displays Abraham's faith against hope. The verse marks the paradox: against hope, in hope. The verse displays the impossibility: nature against him. The verse displays the trust: resting on the promise. The verse displays the object: the numerous seed. Abraham's faith against hope displays the triumph of faith over natural impossibility. The verse displays the paradox: hope beyond hope. The verse displays the ground: God's promise. The verse displays the strength: above natural reason. Abraham's faith against hope, believing in hope, displays the triumph of faith over natural impossibility: trusting God's promise of a numerous seed against all the evidence of nature, his and Sarah's age. The verse displays the heroic strength of Abraham's faith, resting wholly on the divine word above the reach of natural reason, the model of saving faith. The verse continues the description of his unwavering faith (vv.19-21).
19:
"And being not weak in faith, he considered not his own body now dead, when he was about an hundred years old, neither yet the deadness of Sarah's womb:" The witnesses note Abraham's faith not weakened by the impossibilities. On "not weak in faith," the grammatical voices note the strength. Barnes and Robertson note Abraham was not weak in faith — his confidence not shaken by the natural impossibilities.

On "considered not his own body now dead... Sarah's womb," the witnesses note the obstacles overcome. Abraham, about a hundred years old, and Sarah long past childbearing — both "dead" as to procreation; yet Abraham did not let these impossibilities weaken his faith. He weighed them, yet was not overcome by them.

The Reformed Calvin notes the point: faith does not ignore difficulties but rises above them, resting on God's power. The verse displays Abraham's faith unweakened by the impossibilities.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Abraham was not weak in faith — his confidence not shaken (Barnes, Robertson) — though he considered his own body now dead (about a hundred years old) and the deadness of Sarah's womb (long past childbearing), both "dead" as to procreation; yet these impossibilities did not weaken his faith. Calvin notes faith does not ignore difficulties but rises above them, resting on God's power. The verse displays Abraham's faith unweakened. The verse marks the strength: not weak in faith. The verse displays the obstacles: his and Sarah's deadness. The verse displays the triumph: not overcome by them. The verse displays the resting: on God's power. Abraham's faith unweakened displays its strength against the greatest natural obstacles. The verse displays the impossibilities: the dead bodies. The verse displays the confidence: not shaken. The verse displays the foundation: God's power. Abraham's faith unweakened by the impossibilities — his own dead body and Sarah's dead womb — displays the strength of true faith: weighing the difficulties honestly yet not overcome by them, resting on the power of God above all natural impossibility. The verse displays the model of robust faith that faces the obstacles and rises above them, and continues the portrait of Abraham's strong faith (vv.20-21).
20:
"He staggered not at the promise of God through unbelief; but was strong in faith, giving glory to God;" The witnesses note Abraham's strong faith glorifying God. On "staggered not through unbelief," the grammatical voices note the steadiness. Barnes and Robertson note Abraham did not waver or doubt the promise through unbelief — his faith held firm without hesitation.

On "strong in faith," the witnesses note the strength. Abraham was strengthened, made strong, in faith — growing more confident, not wavering.

On "giving glory to God," the witnesses note the fruit. The Reformed Calvin and eastern Theodoret note that faith glorifies God by honoring his power and truth — trusting his promise gives him the glory due his omnipotence and faithfulness. The verse displays Abraham strong in faith, giving glory to God.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Abraham staggered not at the promise through unbelief — not wavering or doubting (Barnes, Robertson) — but was strong (strengthened) in faith, giving glory to God; for faith honors God's power and truth, trusting his promise and so giving him the glory due his omnipotence and faithfulness (Calvin, Theodoret). The verse displays Abraham strong in faith, glorifying God. The verse marks the steadiness: staggered not. The verse displays the strength: strong in faith. The verse displays the fruit: giving glory to God. The verse displays the honor: trusting his power and truth. Abraham strong in faith displays the God-glorifying nature of faith. The verse displays the firmness: no wavering. The verse displays the growth: strengthened in faith. The verse displays the glory: God honored. Abraham strong in faith, giving glory to God, displays the God-glorifying nature of true faith: by trusting the promise without wavering, Abraham honored God's power and faithfulness, giving him the glory due his omnipotence. The verse displays the great truth that faith glorifies God by resting on his word, the deepest worship of the Creator. The verse continues with the ground of his confidence (v.21). By thus giving glory to God in the very act of trusting, Abraham becomes the pattern of all true worship, which honors the Creator most when it leans wholly upon his bare word against the contradiction of sense.
21:
"And being fully persuaded that, what he had promised, he was able also to perform." The brief verse notes the ground of Abraham's faith: full persuasion of God's power. On "fully persuaded," the grammatical voices note the assurance. Barnes and Robertson note Abraham was fully persuaded (completely convinced) — his faith a settled, entire conviction, not a wavering hope.

On "able also to perform," the witnesses note the object. The ground of his faith was God's power: that what God had promised, he was able to perform — Abraham resting on God's omnipotence.

The Reformed Calvin and eastern Theodoret note the essence of faith: trusting not the visible probability but God's power and faithfulness to fulfill his word. The verse displays Abraham fully persuaded of God's power to perform.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Abraham was fully persuaded (completely convinced, a settled entire conviction, not a wavering hope, Barnes, Robertson) that what God had promised he was able also to perform — resting on God's omnipotence. Calvin and Theodoret note the essence of faith: trusting not visible probability but God's power and faithfulness. The verse displays Abraham fully persuaded of God's power. The verse marks the assurance: fully persuaded. The verse displays the object: God able to perform. The verse displays the ground: God's omnipotence. The verse displays the essence: trusting God's power. Abraham fully persuaded displays the settled assurance of true faith. The verse displays the conviction: complete, not wavering. The verse displays the foundation: God's power. The verse displays the trust: in God's faithfulness. Abraham fully persuaded that God was able to perform what he promised displays the settled assurance at the heart of true faith: a complete conviction resting not on visible probability but on the omnipotence and faithfulness of God to fulfill his word. The verse displays the essence of saving faith — confidence in God's power to keep his promise — and the model of the unwavering assurance that honors God. The verse sets up the imputation of this faith for righteousness (v.22). This settled assurance, resting on omnipotence rather than appearance, is the very nerve of the faith that justifies, and the model held out to every believer who must likewise trust the God who keeps his word against all that is seen.
22:
"And therefore it was imputed to him for righteousness." The brief verse draws the conclusion: Abraham's faith imputed for righteousness. The grammatical voices note the inference. Barnes and Gill note that because of this strong, God-glorifying faith (vv.18-21), it was imputed (reckoned) to Abraham for righteousness — the conclusion of the description of his faith.

On "imputed for righteousness," the witnesses note the recall. This restates the key text (v.3, Genesis 15:6) — Abraham's faith, fully described, was reckoned for righteousness.

The Reformed Calvin notes the point: it was this faith — trusting God's mercy and power — that was counted for righteousness, not as a work but as laying hold on God's promise. The verse displays the conclusion: Abraham's faith imputed for righteousness.

The very brief comment matches the briefest verse. The witnesses agree: because of this strong, God-glorifying faith (vv.18-21), it was imputed (reckoned) to Abraham for righteousness (Barnes, Gill) — restating the key text (v.3, Genesis 15:6). Calvin notes it was this faith, trusting God's mercy and power, that was counted for righteousness, not as a work but as laying hold on God's promise. The verse displays the conclusion. The verse marks the inference: therefore imputed. The verse displays the recall: the key text restated. The verse displays the faith: fully described. The verse displays the imputation: reckoned for righteousness. The conclusion displays the imputation of faith for righteousness. The verse displays the reckoning: faith counted. The verse displays the ground: laying hold on the promise. The verse displays the summary: faith imputed. The conclusion that Abraham's faith was imputed to him for righteousness displays the great doctrine of justification by faith, drawn from the full description of his strong, unwavering trust in God's power and promise. The verse displays that it was this faith — not as a meritorious work but as laying hold on God's mercy — that was reckoned for righteousness, the pattern of all who are justified. The verse sets up the application to all believers (vv.23-25). Thus the faith of the patriarch, fully described in its strength and its object, stands as the abiding pattern of the justifying faith by which every believer, laying hold on the divine promise, is reckoned righteous before God.
23:
"Now it was not written for his sake alone, that it was imputed to him;" The witnesses note the application: written for us also. On "not for his sake alone," the grammatical voices note the wider purpose. Barnes, Chrysostom, and Robertson note the record of Abraham's justification was written not merely as history about him, but for the instruction of all believers.

On the application, the witnesses note the design. The Scripture recorded Abraham's imputed righteousness as a pattern and assurance for all who would believe — not a private record but a universal lesson.

The eastern Chrysostom and puritan Henry note the comfort: God's dealings with Abraham are written for our learning, that we might know justification by faith is for us too. The verse displays the application: written not for Abraham alone.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the record of Abraham's justification was written not for his sake alone (not merely history about him, Barnes, Chrysostom, Robertson) but for the instruction of all believers — a pattern and assurance for all who would believe, a universal lesson, not a private record. Chrysostom and Henry note the comfort: God's dealings with Abraham are written for our learning. The verse displays the application: written not for Abraham alone. The verse marks the purpose: for our instruction. The verse displays the pattern: Abraham's example for all. The verse displays the design: a universal lesson. The verse displays the comfort: justification by faith is for us. The application displays the universal relevance of Abraham's justification. The verse displays the record: written for all. The verse displays the assurance: for believers. The verse displays the lesson: for our learning. That Abraham's justification was written not for his sake alone displays the universal relevance of the record: the Scripture preserved the account of his imputed righteousness as a pattern and assurance for all believers, that we might know justification by faith is for us too. The verse displays the comfort of God's word, written for our learning, and sets up the direct application to those who believe on the God who raised Jesus (vv.24-25).
24:
"But for us also, to whom it shall be imputed, if we believe on him that raised up Jesus our Lord from the dead;" The witnesses note the application to believers in the risen Christ. On "for us also, to whom it shall be imputed," the grammatical voices note the promise. Barnes and Robertson note righteousness shall be imputed to us also, on the same terms as Abraham — if we believe.

On "if we believe on him that raised up Jesus," the witnesses note the object of faith. The faith that justifies us is faith in God who raised Jesus from the dead — paralleling Abraham's faith in the God who quickens the dead (v.17). Our faith and Abraham's have the same object: the life-giving God.

The eastern Cyril and Reformed Calvin note the parallel: as Abraham believed God's power to give life, so we believe in the God who raised Christ — the resurrection the ground of our justifying faith. The verse displays the application: righteousness imputed to us who believe on the God who raised Jesus.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: righteousness shall be imputed to us also, on the same terms as Abraham — if we believe on him who raised up Jesus our Lord from the dead (Barnes, Robertson), paralleling Abraham's faith in the God who quickens the dead (v.17); our faith and Abraham's have the same object, the life-giving God. Cyril and Calvin note the parallel: as Abraham believed God's power to give life, so we believe in the God who raised Christ. The verse displays the application to believers. The verse marks the promise: imputed to us also. The verse displays the condition: if we believe. The verse displays the object: God who raised Jesus. The verse displays the parallel: with Abraham's faith. The application displays the same justifying faith for us as for Abraham. The verse displays the imputation: to us who believe. The verse displays the object: the resurrecting God. The verse displays the continuity: Abraham's faith and ours. That righteousness shall be imputed to us also if we believe on him who raised up Jesus from the dead displays the application of Abraham's justification to all believers: our justifying faith, like his, rests on the life-giving power of God, now revealed in the resurrection of Christ. The verse displays the beautiful parallel between Abraham's faith in the God who quickens the dead and our faith in the God who raised Jesus, and sets up the ground of our justification: Christ's death and resurrection (v.25).
25:
"Who was delivered for our offences, and was raised again for our justification." The witnesses note the ground of justification: Christ's death and resurrection. On "delivered for our offences," the grammatical voices note the atonement. Barnes, Clarke, and Gill note Christ was delivered up to death on account of our offences — to make expiation, atonement, satisfaction for our sins (Isaiah 53).

On "raised again for our justification," the witnesses note the resurrection's role. Barnes and Ellicott note the resurrection is connected with our justification: the death is the proper cause of atonement, the resurrection its proof and seal (1 Corinthians 15:17 — if Christ be not raised, ye are yet in your sins). The resurrection proved the atonement accepted and is the ground of our hope.

On imputed righteousness, the witnesses divide. The wesleyan-arminian Clarke argues that it is by Christ's death (not his obedience to the moral law) that we are saved — denying imputed active obedience; the Reformed tradition (Gill) holds Christ's righteousness, including his obedience, imputed. The verse displays the two pillars: delivered for our sins, raised for our justification.

The witnesses agree Christ was delivered for our offences (to make atonement, satisfaction for our sins, Isaiah 53, Barnes, Clarke, Gill) and raised again for our justification — the death the proper cause of atonement, the resurrection its proof and seal (1 Corinthians 15:17, the resurrection proving the atonement accepted and grounding our hope, Barnes, Ellicott). On imputed righteousness they divide: Clarke argues it is by Christ's death, not his obedience to the moral law, that we are saved (denying imputed active obedience); the Reformed Gill holds Christ's righteousness, including his obedience, imputed. The verse displays the two pillars. The verse marks the atonement: delivered for our offences. The verse displays the resurrection: raised for our justification. The verse displays the debate: imputed obedience or not. The verse displays the seal: the resurrection proving the atonement. The two pillars display the foundation of justification in Christ's death and resurrection. The verse displays the death: for our sins. The verse displays the resurrection: for our justification. The verse displays the proof: the atonement accepted. Christ delivered for our offences and raised for our justification displays the two pillars of the gospel: his death the atonement for our sins, his resurrection the proof and seal that the atonement was accepted and the ground of our justification. The verse displays the inseparable connection of the cross and the empty tomb in our salvation, closing the great argument on Abraham and justification by faith, and grounding our justifying faith in the crucified and risen Christ. The verse leads into the fruits of justification (ch. 5).
